role_summary: Survivor of the January 10, 1966 KKK firebombing that killed her husband Vernon Dahmer, Forrest County NAACP president; later elected Election Commissioner of District 2, Forrest County (1992).
ai_reading: Counting how often each interview surfaces among the top neighbors of Dahmer's passages, her most frequent neighbor is her son Vernon Dahmer, Jr. (entry 121, 19 appearances), followed by Rosie Head (entry 111, 11), Clarence Magee (entry 21, 9), Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96, 8), and Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92, 8); apart from her son, the cluster is Hattiesburg and Forrest County voter-registration witnesses rather than family. The sharper observation comes from the single highest-cosine passage matches, where her son does not appear in the top ten at all: the tightest individual matches are to Rosie Head (cosine 0.73) and Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (0.73), both Mississippi registration narratives of night-riding and courthouse refusal. The embedding ties her testimony to her son by sheer breadth of overlap, yet locates its closest single moments in the broader survivor record. On the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis she normalizes to +0.95, the second-deepest Southern reading among the 136 interviews (only John and Jean Rosenberg, entry 61, read more Southern). On the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis she normalizes to +0.80, fourth-most toward Tactical Pragmatism in the corpus: a survivor account whose recorded scenes are a single county's voter-registration ordeal reads, to the embedding, as a record of concrete on-the-ground tactics rather than broad strategic framing. In the influence graph she has no in-edges (no other interviewee in the corpus names her) and one out-edge, to her son.
biographical_paragraph: Before dawn on January 10, 1966, Ku Klux Klansmen firebombed the Dahmer home and adjacent store in Kelly Settlement near Hattiesburg, Mississippi; Ellie Dahmer got her children out of the burning house while her husband Vernon, then president of the Forrest County NAACP, returned fire to cover the family's escape [src: 2]. Vernon died that day of burns and smoke-inhalation damage to his lungs; the couple's daughter Bettie was burned in the attack and was ten and a half at the time, a detail Ellie Dahmer fixes by Bettie's February birthday in her oral history [src: 1, 2]. Ellie Jewel Davis had trained as a home-economics teacher, earning a 1947 bachelor's degree before she taught in Jasper, Jones, and Forrest counties and married Vernon Dahmer [src: 1]. Her husband never cast a vote: she recounts that his voter-registration card arrived in time to be buried with him [src: 1]. In 1992 she was elected an election commissioner for District 2 in Forrest County, the county whose registrar had repeatedly turned her away, and she served three terms [src: 1, 3]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 36 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Ellie Dahmer
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked whether her husband Vernon was a registered voter when they went to the courthouse together, she answered that he never got the chance:
interview_snippets.quote: His voter registration card came [back from the Justice Department] in time to be buried with him.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ellie Dahmer
interview_snippets.lead_in: She describes the literacy test the Forrest County registrar, Theron Lynd, used to deny her registration:
interview_snippets.quote: He gave me a card, a 2 by 5 card with the Constitution on part of... some of the Constitution on the card. He told me to read that and tell him what it means. And of course, I read it and I could very well explain it to him. He would tell me, oh, you don't know what you're talking about. You didn't pass.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ellie Dahmer
interview_snippets.lead_in: On living through the threats that came while the family hosted NAACP meetings and pressed to register:
interview_snippets.quote: We were getting threats. I broke out all the way up here in my neck, all the way down. Yes, I was afraid. Nobody wants to die.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ellie Dahmer
interview_snippets.lead_in: She recounts replacing the household curtains so passing cars could not see the family moving inside at night:
interview_snippets.quote: I had made curtains for the living room in the den. When we went out there one night, it could see through the curtain. You see vehicles through that, so I'd take all of them down and bring in a line of them, so you couldn't see through them. It's a blackout curtain, just for the protection of that.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ellie Dahmer
interview_snippets.lead_in: Decades later, serving as an election commissioner in the same county that had refused her the vote, she reflected on the work:
interview_snippets.quote: It was a wonderful feeling to be able to help with something that they had to die in the arm. And I made sure that nobody else was denied. We followed the law.

interview_snippets.speaker: Vernon Dahmer, Jr.
interview_snippets.lead_in: Her son Vernon Dahmer, Jr. describes the place his mother held in the family after the attack and her decision to stay in Kelly Settlement:
interview_snippets.quote: She's the leader of our family. Even though we were all men, she went through all this, and we never overshadow her. You may notice that. We don't do that. And as a result of what happened to our family, she chose not to leave. She chose to stay here because she had those two kids.

movement_context: The January 10, 1966 firebombing of the Dahmer home and adjacent Kelly Settlement general store was a direct response to Vernon Dahmer Sr.'s January 9, 1966 WDAM-TV announcement that the Forrest County NAACP would accept poll-tax payments at his store on behalf of Black would-be voters who feared retaliation at the Forrest County Courthouse poll-tax office in downtown Hattiesburg [src: 2, 4]. The attack was planned and ordered by White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan Imperial Wizard Samuel Holloway Bowers, the same Bowers who had ordered the June 1964 Neshoba County murders of Andrew Goodman, Michael Schwerner, and James Chaney [src: 2, 3]. In the predawn hours of January 10, a convoy of Klansmen drove to the Dahmer property in Kelly Settlement, threw firebombs through the windows of the house and store, and opened fire on the family as they fled; Ellie Dahmer carried the couple's youngest children to safety while Vernon returned fire to cover their escape, sustaining the smoke-inhalation and lung-damage injuries that killed him the next day [src: 2, 4]. Through the late 1960s and into the 1970s Ellie Dahmer continued the Forrest County NAACP chapter work her husband had led, holding the chapter together through the four state-court attempted prosecutions of Bowers and his co-conspirators between 1968 and 1971 (the four state trials each ended in hung juries or mistrials, the Mississippi state-court system delivered no conviction against Bowers for the Dahmer murder during the lifetime of any of the original 1966 case officers) [src: 3]. The Forrest County chapter presidency she helped sustain through that interregnum passed to Clarence Magee (catalog entry 21) for his 1970-1974 first term, and then to subsequent leaders before Magee's return in 2001 [src: 5, 6]. The institutional resolution came only with the 1998 Forrest County state-court conviction of Bowers for the Dahmer murder, in which Ellie Dahmer testified as the principal eyewitness to the firebombing; Bowers received a life sentence and died in prison in 2006 [src: 2, 3].
legacy_and_reception: Ellie Dahmer's August 1998 testimony at the Forrest County, Mississippi state-court trial of Samuel Bowers for the murder of her husband (the fifth attempted prosecution after the four 1968-1971 state trials had ended in hung juries) was the principal eyewitness testimony that produced the conviction; the jury returned a guilty verdict on August 21, 1998, thirty-two years after the firebombing, and Bowers was sentenced to life in prison [src: 2, 3]. The Federal Bureau of Investigation's Jackson Field Office had reopened the cold case in the mid-1990s in coordination with the Mississippi Attorney General's Office, and the FBI's institutional retrospective on the case names Ellie Dahmer's continuing willingness to testify across more than three decades as central to the eventual conviction [src: 2]. In 2003 the Mississippi Civil Rights Hall of Fame inducted Ellie Dahmer (alongside her late husband Vernon) for her role in sustaining the Forrest County NAACP through the post-1966 interregnum and for her testimony in the federal- and state-court prosecutions [src: 7]. Through the 2010s and 2020s she has continued public-speaking engagements at Hattiesburg-area schools, the University of Southern Mississippi, and Mississippi Department of Archives and History commemorations, anchoring the annual January 10 remembrance of the 1966 attack [src: 4, 7]. Her Library of Congress oral history (recorded 2013, accessioned to the American Folklife Center as AFC 2010/039) is the most extensive published first-person account of the firebombing and the long campaign of prosecutions [src: 1].

gallery.alt: Vernon Dahmer Sr. funeral, Shady Grove Baptist Church, Kelly Settlement, Hattiesburg, Mississippi, January 15, 1966
gallery.caption: The graveside funeral service for Vernon Dahmer Sr. at Shady Grove Baptist Church in Kelly Settlement near Hattiesburg, Mississippi on January 15, 1966, five days after his death from smoke-inhalation injuries sustained in the January 10 White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan firebombing of the Dahmer home and general store. Ellie Dahmer (the widow) carried the couple's youngest children to safety during the predawn attack and went on to sustain the Forrest County NAACP chapter through the post-1966 interregnum, the four 1968-1971 hung-jury state trials, and the eventual 1998 successful Forrest County state-court prosecution of White Knights Imperial Wizard Samuel Bowers. Photograph from the Mississippi Department of Archives and History civil rights collection.
gallery.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Mississippi Department of Archives and History)

gallery.alt: Forrest County Courthouse and Confederate Monument, Hattiesburg, Mississippi
gallery.caption: The Forrest County Courthouse and Confederate Monument in Hattiesburg, Mississippi (NRHP Hub City Historic District contributing property 7, reference 80002237). The 1966 Forrest County poll-tax office that Vernon Dahmer Sr. had announced his Kelly Settlement store would replace was located in this courthouse; the August 1998 state-court trial in which Ellie Dahmer testified against Samuel Bowers also took place in this building, and her 1992 election as Election Commissioner of District 2 placed her on the county election-administration apparatus housed here.
